calendarNotes:
Fairfax County Public Schools begins the 2025-2026 school year on August 25, 2025. Spring Break runs March 30–3, 2025, and the school year ends June 17, 2025 — one of the latest last days among Virginia's large districts. FCPS's later end date reflects the district's use of teacher workdays and the school board's calendar structure. Families coordinating with D.C. Public Schools or Maryland's Montgomery and Prince George's Counties should check each calendar separately — spring break alignment in the D.C. metro varies year to year.

Fairfax County Public Schools is one of the most culturally and religiously diverse school districts in the United States, and its academic calendar reflects that diversity in ways unusual for a public school system. In addition to the standard federal holidays, FCPS observes Rosh Hashanah (September 12) and Yom Kippur (September 21) as school holidays — practices more common in Northern Virginia than in most other regions of the country. Families accustomed to these days being workdays in other states may need to adjust childcare planning accordingly.

FCPS's location in one of the highest-cost housing markets in the country means many families are recent transplants from other states or other countries. The district serves a large population of federal government employees, defense contractors, and technology workers — many of whom have flexible or remote work schedules. The Northern Virginia technology corridor (centered around Tysons, Reston, and Dulles) generates a steady stream of new residents, and FCPS adjusts school boundaries as development expands. Families who have recently moved should verify their assigned school with the district before the school year begins.

The D.C. metro area's Spring Break is a major travel corridor moment — Reagan National and Dulles International airports both see elevated traffic during the last week of March. Cherry blossom season in Washington D.C. typically peaks in late March or early April, overlapping directly with FCPS's Spring Break. Families planning a D.C. visit during the break should book accommodations several months in advance and expect heavy crowds on the National Mall.
